participants.reasoning: The extracted participant description refers to a much larger longitudinal cohort with multiple CBT subgroups and different traumatic-event distributions, whereas the official study describes a much smaller escitalopram/placebo sample with specific baseline CAPS data and eligibility criteria.
participants.extracted: Adults aged 18–70 years consecutively admitted to the Hadassah University Hospital emergency department after potentially traumatic events. Of 4,743 screened, 1,996 met DSM-IV PTSD criterion A and were interviewed, 1,502 had acute PTSD symptoms, 756 attended clinical interviews, and 397 met acute PTSD criteria and were invited for treatment. The current longitudinal analysis included 957 participants with data at 10 days and at least two additional time points; mean age was 36.29 years (SD 12.04), and 51% were male. The cohort was primarily exposed to motor vehicle accidents (84.1%), followed by terrorist attacks (9.4%), work accidents (4.4%), and other incidents (2.0%). Of the analytic sample, 125 received CBT (49 PE, 41 CT, 35 delayed PE).

interventions.reasoning: The extracted intervention describes early trauma-focused CBT (PE, CT, delayed PE), but the official intervention is escitalopram versus placebo in an emergency department setting.
interventions.extracted: Early treatment with trauma-focused cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) delivered soon after trauma exposure, including Prolonged Exposure (PE), Cognitive Therapy (CT), and delayed PE; participants were also randomized to escitalopram or placebo, but the review-relevant intervention here is early CBT.

Intervention regimen: 10 mg tablets: 1 tablet daily for 2 weeks, then 2 tablets daily for 10 weeks
Placebo
Number of randomised participants: 23
Time from traumatic event to first intervention administration (SD): 28.91 (5.71) days
Intervention regimen: equivalent placebo scheme

outcomes.reasoning: The extracted outcomes describe short-term PTSD symptom and diagnostic follow-ups plus adverse-event withdrawal, whereas the official outcomes are different measures and domains assessed only at 3 years (PTSD severity/rate, depression, functional disability, and dropout).
outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure PTSD symptom severity and PTSD diagnostic status over follow-up. PTSD symptoms were assessed with the PTSD Symptom Scale interviewer version (PSS-I) at telephone follow-ups and the self-administered PTSD Symptom Scale revised version used during clinical assessments (PSS-SR/PSS-R), with assessments at approximately 10 days, 1 month, 5 months, 7 months, and 15 months after the traumatic event. PTSD diagnosis was assessed with the Clinician-Administered PTSD Scale (CAPS) during the clinical interviews at about 1 month and 5 months, and PTSD status was also evaluated at follow-up time points using the PTSD symptom measures. The study also planned to measure withdrawal due to adverse events for the placebo-controlled treatment comparison.
